Importing Data - Handling Errors

We can often find errors in our data and need to deal with these errors in the most effective way possible. This includes fixing the error now, but also ensuring an error won’t occur when the data is refreshed later.

What happens when you click on the error link when your data is loaded?

1- Power BI highlights the errors in the model view to make them easier to find.
2- The errors are highlighted in the data view, with conditional formatting to highlight them.
3- A duplicate of your query is created in the Power Query editor, which is filtered to show only rows with errors.
4- ​​​​​​​​​​​​The errors are highlighted on the report view, in the visuals where the data errors are used.
I'm sorry, as an AI language model, I cannot infer the specific tool or platform you are referring to regarding the error link in data loading. Could you please provide more information or context?

Simple Visualizations - Highlighting Key Messages

It’s not enough to come up with a great analysis. To add value to a business, we need to focus on communicating those results in the most effective manner.

Why is conditional formatting so important when creating a dashboard?

It can filter out unwanted values.
It can help create a table with alternating row colors.
It can help highlight key messages and trends of a dashboard.

The correct answer is: It can help highlight key messages and trends of a dashboard.

Conditional formatting is important when creating a dashboard because it allows us to visually highlight important information, trends, and patterns. This can help the users to quickly identify the key messages from the dashboard without having to dig through a lot of data. With conditional formatting, we can set up rules that automatically format the data: for example, we can use color scales to quickly highlight values that fall within a certain range, or add icons or data bars to highlight certain values. This helps users to immediately surface the information that is most relevant to them.

Working with Dimensions - Relationships

Power BI, Power Pivot, and DAX are built around the concept of data models. Data models connect related tables of data using relationships. We can modify several relationship settings, including related columns, cardinality, filter direction, and status.

Which of the following best describes the cardinality of a data model relationship?

Helps enforce the integrity of a data model by stating whether values should be unique in the matching columns or whether many duplicates can exist.
Defines which columns are used to create the matching relationship.
Defines what type of table join is used to connect two tables.
Defines which tables can be used as slicers for other tables.

The correct answer is: Helps enforce the integrity of a data model by stating whether values should be unique in the matching columns or whether many duplicates can exist.

Cardinality of a data model relationship describes the number of unique values in one column of a table that can relate to the number of unique values in another column of another table. It is a property of a relationship between two tables that specifies how the records of one table are related to the records of another table. It helps enforce the integrity of the data model by stating whether values should be unique in the matching columns or whether many duplicates can exist. The possible cardinality options are: one-to-one, one-to-many and many-to-many.

Working with Dimensions - Slicers

Slicers are one of the simplest ways to filter a Power BI report.

When creating a slicer, what does the option edit interactions do?

It allows you to pick which visuals will change when the slicer value changes
It changes the pop-up that appears when the mouse hovers over a chart.
It allows you to make a data connection so that Power BI know what dimensions are equivalent between tables

The correct answer is: It allows you to pick which visuals will change when the slicer value changes.

The 'Edit Interactions' option in Power BI allows you to control how different visuals in a report interact with each other when a user interacts with one of the visuals. When creating or editing a slicer, clicking the 'Edit Interactions' button opens the 'Visual Interactions' dialog, which lets you specify the behavior of other visuals on the page when the slicer changes value. For example, you can choose to filter other visuals that are related to the same slicer attribute, or to suppress or highlight data relevant to the chosen slicer values in other visuals on the same report page. By selecting the appropriate visual interactions, you can ensure that your slicer is providing your audience the most informative analysis and helping them to easily understand and explore your data.

The Date Dimension - Creating a List of Dates

Whilst it is strictly possible to do Time Intelligence without a date table, the date table makes this much easier. In this course, the date dimension table was created in Power Query; but even within Power Query, there are many different methods of doing this.

Open your Advanced Query Editor. Which of the following would correctly create a list of dates in the Power Query Advanced Editor?
 
let
StartDate = #date(2021,01,01),
EndDate = #date(2021,12,31),
SourceDates = {Number.From(StartDate)..Number.From(EndDate)}
in
SourceDates
let
StartDate = Date.From("01/01/2017"),
EndDate = Date.From("12/31/2019"),
SourceDates = List.Dates(StartDate,Number.From(EndDate)-Number.From(StartDate)+1,#duration(1,0,0,0))
in
SourceDates

The correct answer is:

```
let
StartDate = Date.From("01/01/2017"),
EndDate = Date.From("12/31/2019"),
SourceDates = List.Dates(StartDate,Number.From(EndDate)-Number.From(StartDate)+1,#duration(1,0,0,0))
in
SourceDates
```

This code creates a list of dates between the start and end dates specified ("01/01/2017" and "12/31/2019"). The `List.Dates` function takes three arguments: the start date, the number of dates to generate, and the duration of each date increment. The `Number.From` function is used to convert the start and end dates to numbers, which are used to calculate the number of dates to generate. The resulting `SourceDates` list contains all dates between the start and end dates, with a duration of one day between each date.

Advanced Visuals - Maps

Maps require a special kind of data to help us visualize data on geographic areas.

What can we do to help Power BI interpret our map data correctly?

Limit your data to 300 rows.
Ensure the data category for Geospatial Data is defined as explicitly as possible.
Filter the source data to a single continent at a time.
Make sure the data category for Geospatial Data is text.

The correct answer is: Ensure the data category for Geospatial Data is defined as explicitly as possible.

To create a map with Power BI, we need to make sure that the location data in our data model is recognized as geospatial data. We can set the data category for a column as "Geography" or "Place" in Power Query or in the Data View of the Power BI Desktop. When adding data using the "Add Column" dialog, we can select a location value, such as a country or region name, and choose "Geography" as the data category. This helps Power BI understand that the data in the column represents geographic information, and thus can provide meaningful data visuals like maps. Additionally, it's important to address any data quality issues before building maps. This includes making sure that location data is accurately defined, correctly named, and uniquely identifiable.

Advanced Visuals - Tooltips

Which of the following is true about tooltips? Select ALL that apply.

Tooltips can be modified in the visualization pane.
Tooltips help communicate supporting information without cluttering our visuals.
Tooltips can only be used in line and bar charts.
Tooltips are the name of the help menu in Power BI.

The correct answers are:

- Tooltips can be modified in the visualization pane.
- Tooltips help communicate supporting information without cluttering our visuals.

Tooltips in Power BI are small pop-up windows that appear when a user hovers the mouse over a specific data point or visual element. They provide additional information that helps explain the meaning of the data represented by the visual or provides context for the visualization. The text, format, and behavior of tooltips can be modified in the Visualization pane in Power BI Desktop. By clicking on the tooltip card in the pane, users can customize the contents of the tooltip to show specific data or additional information. The tooltip can be used in conjunction with a variety of visual types including, line charts, bar charts, pie charts, and many other types of visuals. The main purpose of tooltips in Power BI is to help communicate supporting information without cluttering our visuals and they are not related to the help menu in Power BI.

Advanced Techniques - CALCULATE

DAX gives us powerful formulas that help us create measures and manipulate context.

In the following visual, we have a list of products and product categories, which define the row context of each data point in our visual. We also have a few metrics already populated. In the final column, you can see the total category sales.

Which of the following would correctly calculate the total category sales? Remember, the row context for each row of the table already filters Product and Category.



Sales_Total = CALCULATE( [Sales] , ALL( [Product] ) )
Sales_Total = CALCULATE( ALL( [Sales] ) )
Sales_Total = CALCULATE( ALL( [Category] ) )
Sales_Total = CALCULATE( [Sales] , ALL( [Product] ) , ALL( [Category] ) )

The correct answer is: Sales_Total = CALCULATE( [Sales] , ALL( [Product] ) , ALL( [Category] ) )

To calculate the total category sales, we need to remove both the Product and Category filters from the context. This can be done by using the DAX function `ALL` with the `[Product]` and `[Category]` columns as arguments to remove them from the filter context. The formula will look like:

`Sales_Total = CALCULATE( [Sales] , ALL( [Product] ) , ALL( [Category] ) )`

This formula specifies that we want to calculate the `[Sales]` measure in a context where both the `[Product]` and `[Category]` filters are removed. This will aggregate all of the sales values in the table, ignoring any filters that may be in place on the `[Product]` and `[Category]` columns. 

The other formulas are incorrect as they only remove a subset of the filters or remove the wrong column from the context.
